title: How the Ponte Vecchio Survived World War II - Prayers & Piazzas
highlights:
1345. But on a wartime night in August of 1944, the historic bridges of Florence were bombed, destroyed as German forces fled the city.  ...    central part of the city anyhow. Demolished in its place, however, were adjacent historic buildings and streets, leaving enough devastation and rubble to render the bridge useless to liberating Allied forces, who would arrive the  ...    Ultimately, Kesselring made the final call: destroy the bridges, save the Ponte Vecchio but cause enough periphery damage to the streets and homes leading up to the old bridge to render it impassable. And that is exactly what happened on the night between August 3 and 4, 1944, during Operation Feuerzauber (magic fire), under Kesselring’s command. title: History of the Vasari Corridor | Florence’s Hidden Passage
highlights:
A car bomb detonated by the Sicilian Mafia damages the corridor, prompting significant restoration efforts.  ...    corridor was constructed to be a hidden and secure route. The walkway extends over the Ponte Vecchio and offers stunning views of Florence, including its iconic landmarks like Piazza della Signoria and the Palazzo Vecchio. A  ...    During Adolf Hitler's visit to Italy, the Vasari Corridor was altered for his convenience. It is believed that some of the central windows in the Vasari Corridor were specially enlarged to offer Hitler an impressive view of the city,  ...    Additionally, many paintings were moved to the Uffizi for protection from bombings.

### Night of the Bridges Year: 1944 During the 'Night of the Bridges' on August 3-4, retreating Nazi forces blew up all Florence's bridges to hinder the advancing Allied troops preparing to liberate the city.
